5.1: The command to be imitators of God (mimētai tou theou) is unique in the NT and striking against the Greco-Roman background. Greek and Roman writers did speak of imitating the gods, but the gods of Homer and Hesiod modeled deception, rivalry, and lust — traits Paul explicitly forbids in the preceding chapters. The Jewish critique of pagan religion was precisely that its gods were immoral. Paul inverts the frame: God himself is the pattern because he is love and light, not because he is powerful. The "dear children" (tekna agapēta) framing activates kinship language; in the ancient Mediterranean, family resemblance was a social and moral category — children were expected to replicate the character of their father.
5.2: "Walk in the way of love" picks up the OT walk-language of covenant faithfulness (halakh), familiar to any synagogue-background reader. The description of Christ's self-offering as "a fragrant offering and sacrifice" (osmē euōdias, prosphora kai thusia) is direct cultic language from the Septuagint — the formula appears dozens of times in Leviticus and Numbers for burnt offerings pleasing to the LORD. Paul applies the entire sacrificial register to Christ's death, signaling that the temple cult's purpose is fulfilled. The original audience, whether Jewish or God-fearing Gentile, would have recognized the priestly register at once.
5.3: Ephesus was among the most significant commercial and religious centers of the ancient world, home to the temple of Artemis — one of the Seven Wonders. The city had a significant sex trade connected to its port economy and to cult practices at various temples. Sexual immorality (porneia) and impurity (akatharsia) were not merely personal failings in this context; they were woven into civic and religious life. Paul's call for these things not to be "even named among you" invokes the Jewish concept of separation from defilement (havdalah) — the holy people maintain a boundary that defines them over against their environment. The identification of covetousness as a form of idolatry was a standard move in Hellenistic Jewish ethics (Philo, Josephus), naming wealth-acquisition as a rival loyalty to God.
5.4: "Foolish talk" (mōrologia) and "coarse joking" (eutrapelia) were recognized categories in Hellenistic moral discourse. Notably, Aristotle in the Nicomachean Ethics praised eutrapelia as a social virtue — the ability to joke with wit and propriety. Paul is not condemning all humor but the degraded form that traffics in obscenity. "Filthiness" (aischrotēs) was a standard rhetorical term for shameful conduct. The contrast — "rather, thanksgiving" — echoes the Hellenistic symposium convention, where gratitude to the host was the proper table-manner. Paul redirects the entire category of social speech toward eucharistia.
5.5: The catalogue of those who have "no inheritance in the kingdom" (5:5) follows a Second Temple Jewish tradition of excluding the immoral from the age to come, seen in texts like 1 Corinthians 6:9–10, 1 Enoch, and the Dead Sea Scrolls. The Qumran community maintained explicit lists of those disqualified from their assembly. The equivalence "greedy person = idolater" reflects Jewish wisdom: covetousness was treated as the worship of wealth rather than God (cf. Matt 6:24). The phrase "kingdom of Christ and of God" is theologically unusual — Paul typically speaks of "God's kingdom" — suggesting an identity of Christ's reign with God's own.
5.6: "Empty words" (kenois logois) likely targets antinomian teaching circulating in Ephesus — possibly an early form of the view that spiritual status is independent of moral behavior. This proto-gnostic position (full development came in the 2nd century) held that the spiritual self was unaffected by bodily actions. Paul counters that God's wrath comes precisely because of "these things." The phrase "children of disobedience" (huioi tēs apeithias) is a Semitic construction: "sons of X" means those defined by X as their essential quality. This is Hebraism adapted into Greek, familiar from Qumran texts ("sons of darkness") and the Synoptic Gospels.
5.7: The brevity of this verse is deliberate — Paul simply restates the boundary: do not share in their practices. The Greco-Roman world operated on patronage and social networks where participation in others' religious and social activities was nearly unavoidable. Paul's instruction cuts against the grain of social obligation; not sharing in the practices of immoral companions was a countercultural stance that would have cost social capital.
5.8: The light/darkness dualism Paul invokes here has deep roots in Second Temple Jewish thought. The Community Rule (1QS) from Qumran divides humanity into "sons of light" and "sons of darkness" under the authority of the Prince of Light and the Angel of Darkness respectively. The War Scroll (1QM) envisions a final cosmic battle between these two camps. Paul's formulation is sharper than mere metaphor: "you were darkness" — not merely "in darkness" — identifies the whole person with the domain of darkness before conversion. The transfer to the domain of light is a change of identity, not just environment.
5.9: Ellicott's observation is important: the best-attested reading is "fruit of the light," not "fruit of the Spirit" (which appears in some manuscripts, likely harmonizing with Gal 5:22). The triad "goodness, righteousness, truth" (agathōsynē, dikaiosynē, alētheia) maps onto Hellenistic virtue categories while being grounded in OT categories of covenant faithfulness. Dikaiosynē here is moral uprightness, not forensic justification — context determines its meaning, and in a list of character qualities it carries the ethical sense. The disputed term (disp=4) is handled by MKT as "righteousness" following the ethical reading.
5.10: "Finding out what pleases the Lord" (dokimazō) draws on the Hellenistic practice of testing coins or metals for purity — dokimazō was used for assaying gold. The Stoic moral tradition also used the concept of testing actions against the rational standard. Paul redirects the standard: not reason alone, but what is "acceptable to the Lord" (euareston tō kyriō). This phrase echoes Hellenistic Jewish usage in Philo and the Wisdom of Solomon, where pleasing God was the criterion for ethical decision-making.
5.11: "Have nothing to do with the fruitless deeds of darkness, but rather expose them" (elegchō) — the verb carries a legal and public dimension in Greek usage: to reprove, convict, or bring to light in a judicial sense. It is used in contexts of formal accusation and public rebuke. This goes beyond personal avoidance; the community is to actively bring the deeds of darkness into the light of public exposure. In the ancient city, shame and honor were publicly enforced; bringing shameful conduct to light was itself a social sanction.
5.12: The secrecy of certain pagan worship was part of its self-presentation as elite mystery knowledge — the mystery cults of Eleusis, Isis, and Mithras all involved secret rites whose content initiates were forbidden to disclose. Paul identifies the secrecy itself as evidence of shame: things that must be hidden are things whose nature cannot bear public scrutiny. The contrast with Christian practice (which Paul urges to "expose") is pointed.
5.13: The logic here exploits the Greek understanding of light as the agent of revealing: in the physical world, objects become visible only when light falls on them; what is in darkness is invisible. Paul applies this to moral reality: the Christian community's life of goodness, righteousness, and truth functions as a kind of ambient light that renders hidden practices visible by contrast. This is a practical mechanism of cultural witness, not only a spiritual metaphor.
5.14: The citation formula "it is said" (legei) without an OT reference suggests Paul is quoting something other than Scripture — most likely an early Christian hymn, possibly used at baptism. The content echoes Isaiah 26:19 ("your dead will live") and Isaiah 60:1 ("Arise, shine, for your light has come"), but it is not an exact quotation of either. The early church regularly composed hymns that wove together scriptural themes (cf. Phil 2:6–11; Col 1:15–20). The address "sleeper" and "rise from the dead" maps onto the baptismal narrative: the candidate descends into the water (death) and rises to new life, and Christ shines on them as their new reality.
5.15: The exhortation to "walk carefully" (akribōs) as the wise, not as fools, draws on the Jewish "two ways" tradition prominent in Proverbs 1–9, Psalm 1, and Deuteronomic literature: there is a way of life and a way of death, wisdom and folly, and the fundamental orientation of one's whole life is at stake in the choice. In Second Temple literature this framework is developed extensively in the Wisdom of Solomon and Sirach. The Didache (early 2nd century) opens with exactly this structure, suggesting it was a standard catechetical framework. Paul invokes it here with eschatological urgency.
5.16: "Making the most of every opportunity" (exagorazomenoi ton kairon) uses the market term exagorazō ("buy up") applied to kairos — the opportune moment as distinct from chronos (clock time). In Hellenistic rhetoric, kairos was the decisive moment that required the prepared speaker or actor; Ecclesiastes uses a similar concept (3:1–8, "a time for everything"). Paul combines this with apocalyptic urgency: "because the days are evil" signals that the present age is under hostile occupation and the opportunities for good are limited. The same phrase appears in Colossians 4:5, suggesting it was Paul's standard formulation.
5.17: "Do not be foolish" (aphron) is stronger than the "unwise" of verse 15; aphron appears in the LXX for those who deny God (Ps 14:1) and in Luke's parables for the man who builds barns without reference to God (Luke 12:20). Understanding "what the Lord's will is" (thelēma tou kyriou) is the defining mark of the wise person in Pauline ethics. The Stoic tradition located wisdom in understanding the logos/rational order; Paul locates it in the personal will of the Lord, which requires revelation and relationship rather than philosophical reasoning alone.
5.18: The contrast between wine and Spirit directly addresses the ecstatic religious practices of Ephesus. The Dionysiac cult (worship of Dionysus/Bacchus, god of wine) involved ritual drunkenness as a means of divine possession; the thiasoi (cultic associations) were common in Ephesian civic life, and drunken ecstasy was considered a form of divine encounter. Paul does not deny that drunkenness produces altered states; he denies that these states represent authentic encounter with God, contrasting them with genuine Spirit-filling. The Acts narrative hints at this when Pentecost observers mistake Spirit-filling for wine (Acts 2:13).
5.19: The three categories — "psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs" (psalmoi, hymnoi, ōdai pneumatikai) — likely reflect distinct forms of early Christian worship. Psalms were probably the biblical Psalter, foundational to Jewish worship in synagogue and home. Hymns were composed pieces of praise, possibly including the Christ-hymns embedded in Paul's letters (Phil 2:6–11; Col 1:15–20). "Spiritual songs" (ōdai pneumatikai) likely refers to spontaneous Spirit-inspired utterance, the sung equivalent of tongues. The Qumran community also composed original hymns (the Hodayot scroll) alongside the biblical Psalms, suggesting this dual practice was not entirely novel.
5.20: The call to "give thanks always for everything" (eucharistountes pantote hyper pantōn) appropriates the language of eucharistia — a term loaded with social obligation in the Hellenistic world. In patron-client relationships, gratitude (charis given, eucharistia returned) was the binding social currency; failing to give thanks to a patron was a serious breach. Paul universalizes this as the posture of the Christian toward God as ultimate patron and Father. The theological move is significant: all goods come from God (not from human patrons), so gratitude runs vertically rather than horizontally.
5.21: This verse is the structural hinge of the household code section. In the Greco-Roman household (oikos), the hierarchy was assumed and legally encoded: the Roman patria potestas gave the father legal authority over wife, children, and slaves. The Hellenistic household management tradition (Aristotle's Politics 1.1, Hellenistic Jewish writers like Philo and Josephus) treated the household as a microcosm of the state. Paul's insertion of "submitting to one another" (hypotassomenoi allēlois) before the specific household instructions frames the entire section under mutual subordination — a move that subverts the hierarchical logic even as the hierarchy is addressed. The motivation — "out of reverence for Christ" — displaces the social system's self-justifying logic.
5.22: Paul's instruction to wives to "submit to your own husbands as to the Lord" follows the Hellenistic household code form but with a crucial difference. In Aristotle's Politics and in standard Greco-Roman household management literature, the husband's authority was natural and self-justifying — wives were subordinate because of their nature. Paul's ground is Christological: the husband's headship is analogous to Christ's headship, which means it is defined by what Christ does in the following verses. The "own" (idios) applied to "husbands" limits the scope — this is not a general subordination of all women to all men, but of wives within the specific covenant of marriage.
5.23: The head-body metaphor for Christ and the church draws on Paul's earlier development in Ephesians 1:22–23 and 4:15–16. In 1 Corinthians 11:3 Paul also uses kephalē (head) of the husband-wife relationship, though there he links it to creation order. The term kephalē in Greek carried connotations of authority and leadership; the claim that it meant only "source" (as in some modern interpretations) is philologically contested. More significant here is Paul's addition of "the Savior of the body" — the husband's headship is defined by analogy with a Savior who gave himself sacrificially. The definition of leadership as salvation rather than dominion is Paul's distinctive contribution.
5.24: The qualification "in everything" (en panti) would have been heard against the backdrop of Roman household law, where a husband's authority was near-absolute. Paul invokes the church-Christ analogy as the regulating principle: as the church submits to Christ, so wives to husbands. But the analogy cuts both ways — the entire following section defines what Christ actually does for the church, which then defines what the husband is obligated to be. The "everything" is constrained by what Christ-like headship actually requires.
5.25: "Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her" — the comparison is extraordinary by any ancient standard. Greco-Roman moral philosophy did speak of husbands' obligation to care for their wives, but the normative frame was beneficent authority, not self-sacrifice. The Stoic Musonius Rufus (roughly Paul's contemporary) argued for mutual fidelity in marriage as a philosophical position, but nothing in Hellenistic household ethics matched the command to love with the self-giving love of the Crucifixion as the standard. Paul has taken the form of the household code and loaded it with content that transforms its logic.
5.26: "Cleansing her by the washing with water through the word" (loutron tou hydatos en rhēmati) is widely understood as a reference to baptism. In early Christian practice, baptism was accompanied by a spoken word — either a baptismal formula, a creedal confession, or a proclamation of the gospel. The combination of water and word echoes Jewish purification practice (immersion in the mikveh) and also the Greek tradition of ritual washings before sacred events. The novelty is the identification of Christ as the one who performs the cleansing through the community's baptismal practice.
5.27: The image of presenting the bride is drawn from the ancient wedding ceremony. In Greek and Roman practice, a paranymphos (the equivalent of a best man) would escort the bride to the groom and formally present her. Paul applies this image to Christ, who presents the church to himself — in an unusual doubling, Christ is both the groom who receives the bride and the paranymphos who prepares her. The language of "without stain or wrinkle" (mē echousa spilon ē rhytida) echoes the inspection of a sacrificial animal for blemish and also the presentation of a bride as unblemished. The combination of sacrifice and marriage imagery is deliberate.
5.28: The care of one's own body was a well-established topic in Hellenistic ethics. The Stoics insisted on the duty of self-preservation as part of natural law; Epictetus distinguished between what is "up to us" (the soul) and our body, which we must maintain as a social responsibility. Paul uses this common-ground assumption — no one hates his own body — as a moral lever to argue for the husband's obligation to love his wife, since she is, in the analogy, part of his own body. The argument presupposes the "one flesh" union of marriage (Gen 2:24, quoted in v.31).
5.29: The two verbs "nourish" (ektrefō) and "cherish" (thalpō) describe aspects of bodily care. Ektrefō was used for raising children to maturity; thalpō carried the warmth of nestling — both words are used in nursing and parental care contexts. The application "just as Christ does the church" extends the metaphor to Christ as the one who feeds and warms his body. This would have resonated with the Eucharistic practices of the community: Christ literally feeds the church in the common meal.
5.30: "We are members of his body" (melē esmen tou sōmatos autou) invokes Paul's body-of-Christ ecclesiology (1 Cor 12, Eph 4:15–16). In Greek medicine (Hippocratic tradition) and philosophy, the body was understood as an integrated whole whose parts were interdependent; injury to one member affected all. Paul's use of this image gave early Christians a somatic, not merely organizational, understanding of their relationship to Christ and each other.
5.31: The quotation of Genesis 2:24 — "a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh" — was foundational in Jewish marriage law. The rabbis debated its implications extensively: did it prohibit polygamy (contested)? Did it establish the indissolubility of the bond? In the Dead Sea Scrolls the Damascus Document (CD 4:20–21) uses Genesis 2:24 to argue for monogamy. Jesus quotes the same verse in his teaching on divorce (Matt 19:5). Paul quotes it here in a pesher-style application that reaches beyond the legal question to the deeper referent revealed in Christ.
5.32: The word mystērion in Paul's usage (especially in Ephesians: 1:9; 3:3–4, 6, 9; 6:19) refers not to something mysterious or obscure but to a divine plan that was previously hidden and is now revealed — a technical sense found also in Daniel 2 (Aramaic rāz) and the Qumran pesher literature. The mystery here is that the "one flesh" union of Genesis 2:24 was always about Christ and the church — the human institution of marriage was a prophetic sign embedded in creation that waited for its revealed meaning. Paul is not allegorizing marriage away; he is claiming that Genesis 2:24 had a referent deeper than human marriage that Genesis itself did not yet identify.
5.33: The practical summary returns from the cosmic analogy to the household. The husband is to love, the wife to "respect" or "fear" (phobētai). In the ancient household code tradition, the appropriate response to a head of household was phobos — not terror but the respectful honor given to authority. The same root describes the children's relation to parents (6:1–2) and slaves' relation to masters (6:5). Paul's use here is a social register word meaning appropriate deference and honor, not emotional fear. The verse deliberately mirrors the asymmetry of the preceding section: the husband's obligation is love (costly, self-sacrificial), the wife's is honor (relational, communal).

6.1: The command for children to obey parents "in the Lord" was set against the backdrop of Roman patria potestas — the father's legal authority over the household, which in earlier Roman law included power of life and death over children (rarely exercised by Paul's time but culturally foundational). Greek paideia (education/formation) centered on learning to honor one's parents as the first institution of civic virtue. Jewish instruction texts (Sirach 3:1–16, Tobit 4:3–4) similarly emphasized filial obedience. Paul grounds his command in both natural law ("for this is right," dikaion) and the specific authority of the Lord — qualifying parental obedience by a higher loyalty.
6.2: The quotation from Exodus 20:12 (= Deut 5:16) is the Fifth Commandment, the pivot of the Decalogue between the God-directed commands (1–4) and the neighbor-directed commands (6–10). Paul's observation that it is "the first commandment with a promise" was a recognized point in Jewish catechesis; the rabbis noted that both honoring parents and releasing the bird's nest (Deut 22:6–7) carried the same promise of long life, leading to discussions about reward. The Decalogue was the foundational catechetical text for both Jewish proselytes and God-fearers who attended the synagogue — the Ephesian Gentile believers who came through synagogue contact would have known it.
6.3: The promise "that it may go well with you and that you may enjoy long life on the earth" slightly modifies the LXX, removing the specific reference to "the land which the Lord your God gives you" — a territorial promise to Israel. Paul universalizes it: "on the earth" (epi tēs gēs) rather than "in the land." This is a significant interpretive move: the covenant promise attached to the commandment is de-territorialized and applied to all members of the new covenant community regardless of ethnic identity. The pattern of citing the Decalogue while universalizing its application was characteristic of Hellenistic Jewish missionary preaching (cf. Philo, Josephus).
6.4: "Fathers, do not exasperate your children" (mē parorgizete ta tekna hymōn) directly addresses the Roman patria potestas tradition in which a father's arbitrary authority was legally protected. The corresponding verse in Colossians 3:21 adds the explanation "lest they become discouraged" — parental harshness produced the psychological damage of chronic discouragement. The command to "bring them up in the training and instruction of the Lord" (paideia kai nouthesia kyriou) commandeers the Greek educational vocabulary: paideia was the entire Greek system of moral formation through literature, music, philosophy, and practice; nouthesia was moral warning and correction. Paul redirects the cultural curriculum from Homeric virtue to the instruction of the Lord.
6.5: Roman slavery encompassed a wide social spectrum: educated slaves served as doctors, teachers, secretaries, and household managers alongside agricultural laborers and mine workers. Roughly a quarter to a third of the population of major cities like Ephesus were enslaved. The philosopher Epictetus was a former slave. In Paul's communities, slaves and free sat at the same eucharistic table — a remarkable social leveling. Paul addresses slaves as moral agents with full spiritual standing, which was itself countercultural. The phrase "masters according to the flesh" (tois kata sarka kyriois) implicitly relativizes their authority: this mastery is bodily and temporary, not ultimate. Christ is the true kyrios.
6.6: "Eyeservice" (ophthalmodouleia) appears to be Paul's own coinage — it is found only here and in Colossians 3:22 in ancient literature. The concept was well understood from household management discussions: Plato (Republic 3.405a) and Xenophon (Oeconomicus) discuss the difference between slaves who work diligently when watched and those who work only when supervised. The compound word crystallizes the problem: service performed only within the field of the master's eye. "Slaves of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart" (apo psychēs) — the contrast is inner motivation vs. external performance, which maps onto the entire Pauline contrast between law-observance and Spirit-led obedience.
6.7: "With good will doing service as to the Lord, and not to people" — the Stoic tradition distinguished between doing one's duty because it is duty (acting rightly) and doing it for external reward or fear. Paul's instruction has a Stoic surface but a different foundation: the good will (met' eunoias) is directed toward the Lord, not grounded in the Stoic sage's self-sufficiency. The reorientation of one's work as service to Christ rather than to human authority was a profound social reframing: the slave who worked for Christ had a dignity no master could grant or remove.
6.8: The assurance that "the Lord will reward each one for whatever good they do, whether they are slave or free" invokes the final judgment as the great equalizer. In the Roman world, social status determined legal outcomes: a free person and a slave who committed the same act received different legal treatment. Paul announces that the eschatological tribunal recognizes no such distinction. The same word for "reward" (komisetai) was used in commercial transactions for receiving back what was owed — Paul frames divine recompense as a debt God will pay, honoring the labor that human law ignored.
6.9: "There is no favoritism with him" (prosōpolēmpsia, literally "face-receiving") directly subverts the patron-client system. In the Hellenistic world, favors, legal outcomes, and honor were distributed according to social status — the "face" (persona) one presented determined what one received. The Jewish tradition rejected this principle in legal proceedings (Lev 19:15; Deut 1:17; 16:19) and applied it to God (Deut 10:17; 2 Chr 19:7). Paul applies it to the master-slave relationship: the master who threatens will face the same impartial judge. The instruction to "do the same things to them" — treating slaves with the same consideration — was radical in the ancient household.
6.10: "Finally, be strong in the Lord and in his mighty power" (en tō kratei tēs ischyos autou) — the vocabulary of power here is drawn from the earlier prayer of 1:19 where Paul uses the same stacked power-terms to describe the resurrection power available to believers. The shift to military imagery in the following verses was natural in a world where Paul was physically chained to a Roman soldier (cf. Acts 28:16, 20). Writing from Roman imprisonment, likely around 60–62 AD, Paul had daily visual access to legionary equipment. The "strength of his might" language also echoes Isaiah 40:26, 29, where the Lord strengthens the weary.
6.11: The "whole armor of God" (panoplia tou theou) — the word panoplia (full armor set) appears in Wisdom of Solomon 5:17–20, where it describes God's own armor in his eschatological battle against the wicked. Paul's use of "of God" is deliberate: the armor is God's own, made available to the believer, echoing Isaiah 59:17 where the LORD himself puts on righteousness as a breastplate and a helmet of salvation. The "schemes of the devil" (methodias tou diabolou) — the word methodeias was used for cunning stratagems in military and rhetorical contexts; it implies a calculating intelligence behind the opposition.
6.12: The catalog of opponents — "rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world, spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms" (archai, exousiai, kosmokratores tou skotous toutou, pneumatika tēs ponērias en tois epouraniois) — reflects Second Temple Jewish cosmology in which the universe is populated by spiritual agents with authority over nations, cosmic domains, and human affairs. Daniel 10:13, 20 names the "prince of Persia" and "prince of Greece" as angelic powers behind earthly empires. The Qumran War Scroll envisions a final battle against these cosmic powers. Paul does not treat them as mere metaphors for human institutions; the "heavenly realms" (en tois epouraniois) is his specific term for the spiritual dimension that intersects with earthly reality (cf. Eph 1:3, 20; 2:6; 3:10).
6.13: "The day of evil" (hēmera ponēra) is an eschatological term referring to any time of crisis or testing — the Psalms speak of "the day of trouble" (Ps 20:1; 27:5), and the prophets of "the day of the LORD" as a time of judgment. Paul's instruction — "having done everything, to stand" (hapanta katergazamenoi stēnai) — reflects the ancient military ideal of holding position; in Greek tactical thinking, maintaining formation under attack was the mark of elite soldiers. "To stand" (stēnai) appears four times in this section (vv.11, 13, 14), suggesting it is the central image.
6.14: The armor description closely follows Isaiah 59:17: "He put on righteousness as his breastplate, and the helmet of salvation on his head." In Isaiah 59 the LORD acts because there is no human intercessor, no one righteous enough to stand in the breach. Paul now calls believers to wear this divine armor — the equipment of God himself. "Belt of truth" (zōsamenoi tēn osphyn hymōn en alētheia) alludes to the Roman soldier's belt (cingulum), which gathered the tunic for movement and supported the other armor. In Isaiah 11:5 the Davidic shoot has "righteousness as the belt around his waist." The "breastplate of righteousness" (thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs) — here dikaiosynē is moral integrity/covenant-faithfulness rather than forensic justification.
6.15: "Feet shod with the readiness of the gospel of peace" echoes Isaiah 52:7 — "How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news" — the messenger racing to announce Israel's liberation from Babylon. The Roman soldier's caligae (hobnailed sandals) were essential for the long marches that made the legions effective; they provided grip and mobility. Paul's spiritual equivalent is the preparedness that comes from knowing the gospel of peace — being grounded and mobile, not paralyzed. The pairing of "gospel" and "peace" in a military context is characteristically Pauline irony: the soldier's weapon is the announcement of peace.
6.16: The shield here is the thureos — the large Roman rectangular shield (as opposed to the small round aspis), large enough to crouch behind and cover the entire body. Ancient incendiary weapons included arrows wrapped in tow and dipped in pitch, then lit; in siege warfare these were a feared weapon. The legions had countermeasures including soaking the thureos in water before battle. The "flaming arrows of the evil one" captures an image familiar to any first-century Mediterranean person who had seen military operations. Faith (pistis) as the shield implies that trust in God's promises is what intercepts the temptation before it takes hold.
6.17: The "helmet of salvation" (perikephalaian tou sōtēriou) is again from Isaiah 59:17 — the LORD's own helmet. The Roman galea (helmet) protected the head in close combat. Paul's distinction in this verse is important: for the belt, breastplate, shoes, and shield, the command is to "stand with" or "take up"; for the helmet and sword, the command shifts to "take" (dexasthe — receive). The helmet of salvation is received from God, not manufactured by the believer. The "sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" — the Roman gladius (short sword) was for close-quarters combat; Paul identifies it as rhēma (the specific spoken word) rather than logos, suggesting the sword is the proclaimed gospel word in the moment of conflict.
6.18: Prayer bookends the armor section: the armor is put on through prayer. "Pray in the Spirit on all occasions with all kinds of prayers and requests" — the language of "all" is piled up four times (dia pasēs proseuchēs... en panti kairō... en pasē proseuchē... hyper pantōn), suggesting totality of orientation. "Be alert" (agrypnountes, literally "sleepless") recalls the watchman imagery in the OT (Isa 62:6; Ezek 3:17) and the disciples' failure to watch in Gethsemane (Mark 14:37–38). The instruction to pray "for all the saints" reflects the early Christian understanding of the universal community as a single body whose members bear one another's needs before God.
6.19: Paul's request for prayer for "boldness" (parrēsia) in proclamation is historically specific: he is in Roman custody, where speaking openly about a gospel that proclaimed a crucified Jew as Lord of the empire was politically dangerous. Parrēsia (literally "all speech," freedom of speech) was a prized civic virtue in Athenian democracy — the right to speak freely in the assembly. In Paul's context it is the freedom to speak the gospel without self-censorship under pressure. The "mystery of the gospel" (mystērion tou euaggeliou) is the same technical term as in 3:3–6 — the previously hidden plan of God now revealed: that Gentiles are co-heirs with Israel in Christ.
6.20: "For which I am an ambassador in chains" (presbeuthō en halusei) is a deliberate paradox. Ambassadors (presbeutai) held inviolable status in the ancient world; the violation of an ambassador was casus belli. Roman, Greek, and Persian diplomatic practice all protected ambassadors from harm. Paul's description of himself as an "ambassador in chains" (halusis — the specific chain linking prisoner to guard) highlights the upended social order: the official representative of the King of kings is imprisoned by the political power that considers itself sovereign. "That I may declare it fearlessly, as I should" — Paul frames his imprisonment not as defeat but as an opportunity that requires divine courage to use rightly.
6.21: Tychicus is named in Acts 20:4 as a delegate from the province of Asia (of which Ephesus was the capital) who accompanied Paul on his collection journey to Jerusalem. He is described in the parallel passage Colossians 4:7 in nearly identical terms — "dear brother and faithful servant and fellow slave in the Lord." The near-verbatim repetition across Ephesians and Colossians supports the inference that Tychicus delivered both letters simultaneously, traveling a circuit through the Asian congregations. The role of letter-carrier in antiquity was more than postal delivery; the carrier was expected to supplement the written letter with oral explanation and news, and to vouch for the sender's circumstances.
6.22: The phrase "that he may encourage your hearts" (parakalese tas kardias hymōn) uses the Pauline word for the ministry of consolation and strengthening (the same root as Paraclete in John's Gospel). In a community anxious about Paul's imprisonment, Tychicus's visit would have served a pastoral function beyond information delivery: he embodied the network of connection that held dispersed Pauline communities together across the empire. The letter itself participated in this function — a written presence where physical presence was impossible.
6.23: The closing benediction follows the standard Hellenistic letter convention of a concluding wish for the recipients' welfare, but with distinctively Pauline theological content. "Peace" (eirēnē) is the Hebrew shalom transliterated into Greek — comprehensive wellbeing, right relationship, wholeness, not merely the absence of conflict. "Love with faith" (agapē meta pisteōs) pairs the two central Christian virtues; the addition "from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" makes the source explicit, locating the benediction within the Trinitarian economy of grace. The letter opened with the same double source (1:2); the closing mirrors the opening.
6.24: "Grace to all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with an undying love" — the final phrase en aphtharsia is striking. Aphtharsia means incorruption or immortality — the same word used for the resurrection body in 1 Corinthians 15:42, 50, 53–54. The closing therefore frames love for Christ in terms of resurrection permanence: a love that participates in the incorruptible life of the age to come. The plural "all" (pasin) suggests the letter was read in a wider circle than a single congregation — the circular letter hypothesis is supported by the absence of "in Ephesus" from several early manuscripts at 1:1. Paul closes with an inclusive address to all who share in Christ's undying love.

1.1: Ephesus was the largest city in the Roman province of Asia (modern western Turkey) — a provincial capital, a major Mediterranean port, and home to the Temple of Artemis, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world. Paul's three-year residence there (Acts 19:8-10) made it the base for evangelizing the entire province. The phrase "in Ephesus," however, is absent from several of the earliest manuscripts (P46, Codex Vaticanus, Codex Sinaiticus), which simply read "to the holy ones who are also faithful in Christ Jesus." Many scholars conclude Ephesians was a circular letter sent to multiple churches in the region, with the place name left blank to be filled in by the letter-carrier for each destination.
1.2: The greeting "grace and peace" (χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη) fuses the standard Greek epistolary greeting (chairein) with the Hebrew shalom (שָׁלוֹם). Paul's adaptation is not merely stylistic — by attributing both to "God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," he places the two on the same footing as the source of the one unified blessing. The formula appears in every Pauline letter and is now so familiar that its novelty is easily missed: a Jewish blessing and a Greek greeting merged into a new form that signals both communities are addressed.
1.3: The opening "Blessed be God" (εὐλογητὸς ὁ θεός) is a Jewish berakah (blessing) formula found throughout the OT and Second Temple literature and in synagogue liturgy. The long sentence that follows (vv. 3–14 in Greek, a single 202-word sentence) is structured like a Jewish prayer of thanksgiving, cataloguing God's acts before making any petition. The phrase "heavenly realms" (ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις) appears five times in Ephesians and nowhere else in Paul — it signals a vertical dimension of reality inhabited by Christ, angelic powers, and now believers. This distinctive spatial frame is central to Ephesians' theology.
1.4: "Holy and blameless" echoes the cultic language of sacrifice — the animals brought to the altar were required to be tamim (without defect, unblemished). Paul transfers this vocabulary from the sacrificial system to the people of God, signalling that what the cult sought through unblemished animals, God purposes in the community through Christ. "Before the foundation of the world" reflects Jewish cosmological thinking, widespread in Second Temple literature, that divine choices precede creation itself (cf. 1 Enoch 39; 4 Ezra 6:6). Election is not a late divine reaction to human events but a pre-creational decision.
1.5: Roman adoption (adoptio) was a deliberate legal act that transferred a person fully into a new family, conferring the adopter's name, citizenship, and inheritance rights — sometimes used by wealthy men without biological heirs to secure a successor. Unlike birth sons, adopted sons were chosen, making adoption in Roman experience an expression of intentional, deliberate relationship. Paul's Gentile readers in Roman Asia Minor would have grasped the force of this immediately: they are not accidental members of the divine family but chosen and formally incorporated into it.
1.6: "The One he loves" (τοῦ ἠγαπημένου) is a Semitic idiom for the uniquely beloved — the same phrase stands behind "my beloved Son" at Jesus's baptism (Mark 1:11) and transfiguration (Mark 9:7). By placing this at the end of the first strophe ("to the praise of his glorious grace, which he has freely given us in the One he loves"), Paul situates all grace within the Father's love for the Son. The grace believers receive is an extension of that prior, eternal love.
1.7: Redemption (ἀπολύτρωσις) was a commercial and legal term for the release of a slave or prisoner of war through a ransom payment (lytron). Manumission — the formal purchase and liberation of enslaved persons — was a familiar legal reality across the Roman world; temples sometimes served as intermediaries holding the purchase price. Paul's audience would have understood the term concretely, not abstractly. The "forgiveness of trespasses" (παραπτωμάτων) uses the standard Jewish covenant term for acts of faithlessness against God's covenant obligations.
1.8: The pairing of "wisdom and understanding" (σοφίᾳ καὶ φρονήσει) echoes the Jewish wisdom tradition, where wisdom (חָכְמָה, hokhmah) and understanding (בִּינָה, binah) are characteristic divine attributes extended to those in covenant relationship (Prov 3:19-20; Wis 8:18). The idea of God "lavishing" these on believers uses abundance language characteristic of Ephesians — God gives beyond measure, in the manner of a great patron with inexhaustible resources.
1.9: "Mystery" (μυστήριον) in Paul's usage must be distinguished from its use in the Greco-Roman mystery cults (Eleusinian, Dionysian, Mithraic), where secret rites were disclosed only through prolonged initiation, and the content was kept hidden from outsiders on pain of divine punishment. Paul's mystērion follows the Jewish apocalyptic tradition, where raz (mystery) refers to divine secrets disclosed to prophetic seers (Dan 2:18-19; 1 Enoch 103:2). The crucial difference: Paul's mystery is now openly proclaimed — what was hidden is now published to all.
1.10: "When the times reach their fulfillment" (πλήρωμα τῶν καιρῶν) is eschatological language for the appointed moment when God's plan reaches completion. Jewish eschatology expected "this age" (עוֹלָם הַזֶּה, olam hazeh) to give way to "the age to come" (עוֹלָם הַבָּא, olam haba); Paul presents the Christ-event as the hinge. The verb "to bring unity to all things" (ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι) literally means "to sum up under one head" — a rhetorical term for recapitulating an argument's main points. Paul uses it for Christ as the cosmic head who draws all scattered reality under his unifying lordship.
1.11: "We who were the first to put our hope in Christ" likely refers to Jewish believers who lived within the messianic expectation of the covenant before the Gentiles were included. The sequence — Jews first, then Gentiles (vv. 11-13) — mirrors Paul's "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" pattern (Rom 1:16). The "predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will" continues the pre-creational election language of v. 4, now with the forensic note that God's plan executes according to his own counsel, not external constraint.
1.12: "For the praise of his glory" closes the first strophe of the berakah (v. 6), the second (v. 12), and will close the third (v. 14). This triple refrain structures the prayer as a three-movement blessing, each movement ending in the same doxological phrase. Repetition of a key phrase at structural intervals is a Hebrew rhetorical device; its presence here reinforces the Jewish liturgical character of the passage.
1.13: Being "sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" imports the commercial and legal practice of sealing (σφραγίζω). Seals authenticated ownership and guaranteed authenticity — a sealed document could not be opened before the appointed time without destroying evidence of tampering. In Jewish tradition, circumcision was described as the "seal" of the Abrahamic covenant (Rom 4:11). Paul now replaces circumcision with the Spirit as the seal of the new covenant community — a seal accessible to all, male and female, Jew and Gentile. This is among Paul's most pointed implicit arguments against requiring circumcision of Gentile believers.
1.14: The "deposit guaranteeing our inheritance" (ἀρραβών) is a commercial law term for earnest money — a down payment that legally binds the full transaction to follow. The word is actually a Semitic loanword in Greek, borrowed from Phoenician commercial practice and widely used in Greco-Roman contracts. The concept is precise: the Spirit is not merely a pledge in an informal sense but a legally binding first installment that guarantees the full inheritance will be delivered. The "until the redemption of those who are God's possession" uses the same manumission language as v. 7.
1.15: Paul's thanksgiving and prayer section follows the standard Hellenistic epistolary form: after the opening salutation, the writer gives thanks for the recipients and reports praying for them (cf. Rom 1:8-10; Col 1:3-4; Phil 1:3-5). This was a courtesy convention in ancient letters, but Paul fills it with theological content. "Your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love for all God's people" — hearing reports of a community's character before visiting them was a standard feature of ancient correspondence, where distance made personal knowledge depend on travelers' reports.
1.16: "Remembering you in my prayers" — Paul participates in the Jewish pattern of fixed prayer times (the tefillah, morning and evening; cf. Acts 3:1; 10:3, 9; Dan 6:10). His intercessory prayer is not spontaneous recollection but structured, regular liturgical memory. The word translated "remembering" (μνείαν ποιούμενος) is a technical phrase for formal memorial — the kind of deliberate recall that shaped Jewish prayer practice.
1.17: "The Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him" — in Jewish thought, the Spirit is the agent of prophetic insight and divine wisdom (Isa 11:2; Num 11:25; Wis 7:7). The goal, "the knowledge of him" (ἐπίγνωσις αὐτοῦ), echoes the covenantal knowledge formula of Jeremiah 31:34: "They will all know me." This is not merely intellectual acquaintance but relational recognition — the goal of covenant life.
1.18: "The eyes of your heart" — in Hebrew anthropology, the heart (lēb / לֵב) was the seat of understanding, will, and decision, not primarily of emotion as in modern Western usage. Proverbs repeatedly addresses the heart as the center of moral cognition (Prov 4:23; 23:7). The "enlightening" of the heart's eyes is a prayer for cognitive and volitional transformation — the capacity to perceive the reality of what God has done. This is a Hebraic framing, not a Platonic or Stoic one.
1.19: "His incomparably great power for us who believe" — Paul begins to pile up power-vocabulary in a sequence of four Greek terms (δύναμις, ἐνέργεια, κράτος, ἰσχύς) that together convey overwhelming force. The dense accumulation echoes Jewish doxological language for divine power (Ps 29:1; Isa 40:26). This power language activates the Exodus background — the "mighty hand and outstretched arm" (יָד חֲזָקָה, yad chazaqah) of God in Exodus is the paradigm for divine rescue.
1.20: The right hand position was the place of highest honor in ancient Near Eastern and Greco-Roman court protocol — the king's most trusted vizier sat at his right hand (cf. Ps 110:1). Paul's statement that God "seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms" presents Christ's exaltation in political terms: he is the supreme royal official of the divine court. The "heavenly realms" again signals the cosmic dimension of this enthronement.
1.21: "All rule and authority, power and dominion" — these four terms reflect the hierarchy of spiritual beings in Second Temple Jewish cosmology (cf. 1 Enoch 61:10; Col 1:16; Rom 8:38). Jewish apocalyptic literature populated the cosmos with ranked angelic and demonic powers, each governing specific domains. Paul's point is not to affirm the cosmology in detail but to use its terms as an exhaustive sweep: Christ is above every member of every tier of the cosmic hierarchy, including powers whose names have not yet been named.
1.22: "Head over everything for the church" fuses two conceptual traditions. In Hellenistic political philosophy, the body-politic had a head (the ruler) and members (citizens); the head-body metaphor was used for the relationship between ruler and state (Philo employs it). In Jewish wisdom tradition, Wisdom was present at creation and pervades all things (Wis 7:24). Paul combines these: Christ as head is simultaneously the cosmic ruler and the source of the body's life — not merely governor but constitutive source.
1.23: "The fullness of him who fills everything in every way" — plēroma (fullness) became a technical term in later Gnostic cosmology, but here it is grounded in the Jewish theology of divine presence. The glory of the Lord "filled" the tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34-35; 1 Kgs 8:10-11); the eschatological hope was that God's presence would fill all the earth (Num 14:21; Hab 2:14). Paul presents Christ's fullness as this universal divine presence, now mediated through the church as his body.

2.1: "Dead in your transgressions and sins" — the category of spiritual death before God is found in Jewish apocalyptic literature (4 Ezra 7:48) and in the language of covenant curse: the covenant partner who abandons the covenant enters the sphere of curse and death (Deut 28). Paul addresses the Gentile Ephesians as those who had no covenant standing before God, and thus no access to the covenant life. This is not biological death or Greek philosophical dissolution of the soul but the Jewish covenantal category of exclusion from God's life-giving presence.
2.2: "The ruler of the kingdom of the air" — Second Temple Jewish cosmology located demonic powers in the lower atmosphere between earth and the moon (cf. 1 Enoch 18-19; Testament of Solomon; Jubilees 10). The "air" (ἀήρ) was understood as the domain of spiritual forces hostile to God, which would have resonated with both Jewish and Gentile readers familiar with Greco-Roman demonology. "The spirit who is now at work in those who are disobedient" — a common Jewish designation for the adversarial cosmic power who animates human rebellion against God.
2.3: "Children of wrath" is a Hebraic idiom — literally "sons of wrath" (υἱοὶ τῆς ὀργῆς) — meaning those who belong to, or are destined for, wrath. The same construction appears at Qumran: "sons of light" and "sons of darkness" are categories of belonging, not biological descent. "Gratifying the cravings of our flesh" uses the sarx (flesh) language that Paul shares with the Qumran community's use of bāśār (flesh) for the sphere of human weakness and moral failure opposed to God's Spirit (cf. 1QS III-IV).
2.4: "But God, being rich in mercy" — the dramatic reversal introduced by "but God" (ὁ δὲ θεός) is the rhetorical pattern of Jewish lament and prayer: extended description of hopeless human condition followed by divine intervention. This pattern structures the Psalms (e.g., Ps 22) and Jewish confessional prayer (Ezra 9; Neh 9). "Rich in mercy" echoes the Jewish confession of God's character: abundant in hesed (covenant-love) and rahamim (merciful compassion) is how God describes himself at Sinai (Exod 34:6) and how Israel prays to him throughout the OT.
2.5: "Made us alive together with Christ" (συνεζωοποίησεν) introduces a cluster of compound verbs with the prefix syn- ("together with") that characterizes Ephesians 2:5-6. Believers are co-quickened, co-raised, and co-seated with Christ. This inaugurated eschatology — the claim that believers already share in the resurrection life — is a Christian adaptation of Jewish resurrection hope, which expected resurrection only at the end of the age. The parenthetical "(it is by grace you have been saved)" echoes the refrain of v. 8.
2.6: "Seated us with him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus" — the realized eschatology here is striking within Jewish categories. Jewish apocalyptic texts described the righteous being exalted to heaven after death or at the end of the age (cf. 1 Enoch 39; 4 Ezra 7); Paul presents this as already accomplished for believers in the present. The spatial claim — that believers' true location is in the heavenly realms even while they inhabit earthly bodies — is Ephesians' most distinctive theological move.
2.7: "The coming ages" — Jewish eschatology distinguished this age (olam hazeh) from the age to come (olam haba); Paul's "ages" (αἰῶνες) in the plural envisions multiple future epochs of divine generosity. The demonstration of God's grace toward the Ephesians is not merely for their benefit but a cosmic exhibit — the church as the display space for God's inexhaustible riches before the watching creation.
2.8: "By grace you have been saved through faith" — the contrast with "works" (v. 9) is not a generic critique of moral effort but a pointed argument about the boundary markers of God's covenant community. In Second Temple Judaism, "works" could specifically denote the acts of Torah that distinguished Israel from the nations (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath, purity regulations). Paul's claim is that faith — not ethnic or ritual boundary markers — is now the sole criterion of inclusion in the covenant community. "This is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God" — the antecedent of "this" is disputed, but the broader point is clear: the entire salvation, not merely the faith, is God's gift.
2.9: "Not by works, so that no one can boast" — boasting (καύχησις) in covenantal privilege was a recognized feature of Second Temple Jewish piety: knowing the Torah, circumcision, ancestry from Abraham were grounds for confidence before God (cf. Rom 2:17-20). Paul's denial of boasting does not target Gentile self-congratulation but specifically the Jewish claim to covenantal superiority over Gentiles. In the new community, neither group has standing that the other lacks.
2.10: "We are God's handiwork" (ποίημα) — the term echoes creation vocabulary. Jewish and Hellenistic writers used poiēma for a work of divine or skilled craftsmanship. Paul's claim is that believers are God's new creation (cf. Isa 65:17; 2 Cor 5:17) — a claim rich with prophetic freight. The works "prepared in advance" echoes predestination language within a covenantal framework: God's purposes are not reactive but deliberate, established before the events themselves.
2.11: "Gentiles by birth… called 'the Uncircumcision'" — akrobystia (uncircumcision) was a term of social contempt in the Jewish world. The Maccabean crisis, in which some Jewish men attempted to reverse their circumcision to compete in Hellenistic gymnasia (1 Macc 1:15), had made circumcision a boundary marker with intense emotional weight. The irony in Paul's phrase "made in the body by human hands" captures the Jewish polemic against idols (made by human hands) and applies it to circumcision — pointing to its merely external, outward character.
2.12: "Excluded from citizenship in Israel" — politeia (citizenship) is Roman political vocabulary for full membership in a civic body with its attendant rights and privileges. Gentiles were political outsiders to Israel's covenant community, with no claim on the Abrahamic, Sinaitic, or Davidic covenants ("the covenants of the promise" — Paul uses the plural deliberately). "Without hope and without God in the world" — the Gentile world had eschatological philosophies (Epicurean, Stoic) but no communal hope grounded in a divine promise to a covenant people. The Greek atheos (without God) was actually used as an insult against early Christians who refused to worship the gods — Paul reclaims it to describe the pre-Christian Gentile condition.
2.13: "Once far away have been brought near" — the rabbinic distinction between "far" (rahōq) and "near" (qārōv) was a technical category for Gentiles (far from God) and Jews (near, because possessing the covenant). The terminology appears in Isa 57:19, which Paul will cite via v. 17. Paul's claim is that Christ has dissolved this foundational soteriological distinction: those who were structurally, categorically far are now brought near — not by becoming Jews, but by the blood of Christ.
2.14: "The dividing wall of hostility" — scholars debate two referents: (1) the soreg, the low stone balustrade in the Jerusalem Temple separating the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts, with inscriptions warning Gentiles that death awaited those who crossed it (two such inscriptions have been found archaeologically, one now in Istanbul); or (2) the Torah itself, functioning as the social and legal barrier between Jew and Gentile. Paul may intend both simultaneously — the Temple's literal wall was itself a physical expression of the Torah's boundary-marking function.
2.15: "Abolishing in his flesh the law with its commands and regulations" — the "ordinances" (dogmata) were the specific legislative provisions of Torah that created and maintained the social barrier between Jews and Gentiles: food laws preventing shared meals, purity regulations governing contact, circumcision marking ethnic membership. Paul does not abolish the moral content of Torah but its function as a community-marking boundary. "To create in himself one new humanity" — a third entity, neither Jew nor Gentile, is Paul's striking claim; not Jewish absorption of Gentiles, not Gentile eclipsing of Jews, but a new creation in Christ.
2.16: "One body" — sōma (body) was also the standard Greek term for a corporate association, a collegium — one of the many voluntary associations (trade guilds, burial societies, religious groups) that organized civic life in Roman cities. Paul's "one body" subverts this familiar social category: the criterion for membership is neither trade, ethnicity, nor devotion to a patron deity, but union with Christ. The reconciliation to God "through the cross" — the cross as instrument of execution was the most shameful death possible in Roman estimation; Paul treats it as the mechanism of peace.
2.17: "Peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near" — Paul cites or paraphrases Isa 57:19, which the rabbis read as addressing Gentiles (far) and Israel (near). The announcement of peace after judgment and exile was the herald's proclamation of Isa 52:7 — the messenger running ahead of the returning King to announce that war is over. Paul presents Christ as both the herald and the content of that peace.
2.18: "Access to the Father by one Spirit" — prosagōgē (access, audience) was the term for being formally presented into the presence of a monarch or patron — a significant privilege requiring an intermediary. In the Roman imperial court, direct access to the emperor was restricted and required a patron to introduce the petitioner. Paul's claim is that through Christ, by the one Spirit, both Jew and Gentile have this royal audience before God — without the ethnic or cultic intermediaries previously required. The tri-personal structure (through Christ, by the Spirit, to the Father) reflects an early Trinitarian pattern of prayer.
2.19: "No longer foreigners and strangers" — the terms paroikoi (resident aliens) and xenoi (foreign visitors) defined precise legal categories in Roman civic life: the paroikos lived permanently in a city but lacked full citizenship rights (no vote, restricted property ownership, limited legal protection); the xenos was a temporary visitor with even less standing. Paul declares that Gentile believers are now politeiai (fellow citizens) with full civic standing and oikeioi (household members) — not guests accommodated in the house but family, with the intimacy and inheritance that entails.
2.20: "Built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets" — in the context of Ephesians, "prophets" most likely refers to both OT prophets who anticipated the Gentile mission (Isa 49:6; Amos 9:12) and NT prophets active in early churches (cf. Eph 3:5; 4:11). The cornerstone (akrogōniaios) was the critical architectural element placed first, whose precise positioning determined the alignment of the entire structure. Excavations of Hellenistic buildings confirm the cornerstone's structural and symbolic importance.
2.21: "A holy temple in the Lord" — the temple metaphor for the believing community appears also in 1 Cor 3:16-17 and 2 Cor 6:16. In Jewish theology, the temple was the unique locus of God's dwelling (the Shekinah). The loss of the first temple and the perceived inadequacy of the second (which lacked the ark, the Urim and Thummim, and the divine fire) generated a strong expectation of a future restored temple in which God would truly dwell again (Ezek 40-48; 11QT at Qumran). Paul presents the church as the fulfillment of this eschatological expectation — not a building but a community.
2.22: The Shekinah (divine presence) had departed from the first temple at its destruction (Ezek 10:18-19; 11:22-23). Second Temple Judaism knew the second temple lacked the ark of the covenant and the visible cloud-glory. The prophetic hope was for God's presence to return in the messianic age (Ezek 43:1-5; Hag 2:6-9). Paul declares this hope fulfilled: God's Spirit now inhabits the community of believers as his dwelling. The "being built together" (συνοικοδομεῖσθε) is a present passive — ongoing construction, not a finished state.

3.1: "The prisoner of Christ Jesus for the sake of you Gentiles" — Paul writes from prison (most likely Rome, c. AD 60-62, though Caesarea and Ephesus are also proposed). In Roman honor-shame culture, imprisonment was a mark of defeat and disgrace; a prisoner's clients would be expected to feel shame by association. Paul inverts this: his imprisonment is "for the sake of" the Gentiles, reframing it through the patron-client framework as honorable suffering on behalf of those he serves. The formula "prisoner of Christ Jesus" (not "prisoner of Rome") asserts that his real captor is Christ — Rome holds his body, Christ holds his allegiance and purpose.
3.2: "The administration of God's grace" — oikonomia (stewardship, household management) is a technical term from Greek household and estate administration. The oikonomos (steward) managed a great household's resources on behalf of the master, with delegated authority and significant responsibility. Paul presents himself as the steward of a divine household secret: he did not invent the mystery of Gentile inclusion but received it as a trust from the divine householder and must administer it faithfully. The term also carries the sense of an ordered plan or dispensation.
3.3: The contrast with mystery-religion esotericism is implicit but important. In the mystery cults (Eleusinian, Dionysian, Mithraic), initiates underwent preparatory stages before receiving secret knowledge, and they were forbidden on pain of divine punishment from revealing the content to outsiders. Paul's mystērion follows the Jewish apocalyptic tradition (Dan 2:18-19; 1 Enoch 103:2; 1QpHab) where raz (mystery) is a divine secret disclosed to selected seers — but disclosed precisely so it can be proclaimed. Paul's mystery is the opposite of esoteric: it is now openly announced to all.
3.4: "When you read" acknowledges that this letter is being read aloud in the gathered assembly — the standard mode of reception for both epistolary and scriptural texts in antiquity. Ancient readers were auditors, and texts were composed with this oral reception in mind. The act of reading together is itself a participation in the unveiling of the mystery Paul describes.
3.5: "Not made known to people in other generations as it has now been revealed" — this does not claim the OT was entirely silent about Gentile inclusion (the OT prophets anticipated it: Isa 49:6; 56:6-8; Amos 9:11-12). The point is that the specific form of that inclusion — Gentiles as co-heirs without becoming Jews, on equal terms through Christ — was not previously disclosed. The full reading of the OT's Gentile horizon was not available until the Christ-event provided the hermeneutical key to unlock it.
3.6: The three compound "together-with" words Paul coins here have no precedent in Greek or Jewish literature: synklēronoma (co-heirs), syssōma (members-together-of-one-body), symmetocha (co-participants in the promise). The novelty of the coinage signals the novelty of the reality. Jewish proselytes were historically incorporated into Israel by adopting its practices and identity; Paul's mystery is co-heirship without assimilation — Gentiles as Gentiles, alongside Jews, in one body.
3.7: "The gift of God's grace given me through the working of his power" — the double genitive ("gift of grace") followed by "working of power" uses two of the key-term pairs that characterize Ephesians' power-language (cf. 1:19). Paul grounds his ministry authorization entirely in divine action, not human qualification. This is consistent with his defensive posture in Galatians and 1-2 Corinthians, where his apostolic credentials were challenged.
3.8: "Less than the least of all the Lord's people" — the Greek superlative of the comparative (elachistoteros) is grammatically an intensified self-deprecation. This is a recognized rhetorical topos in Greco-Roman letter-writing: establishing ethos through exaggerated humility before making a significant claim. Paul employs it also in 1 Cor 15:9 ("least of the apostles") and 1 Tim 1:15 ("worst of sinners"). The convention does not undercut sincerity — it was the form in which genuine humility was expressed in elevated ancient prose.
3.9: "Hidden in God since the ages began" — the phrase echoes Jewish language about divine secrets concealed before creation and disclosed in the end-time (Dan 2:22; 1 Enoch 51:3; Wis 6:22). The hiddenness was not arbitrary but purposeful: the fullness of the divine plan could only be disclosed at the point of its execution. The "administration" of this mystery is again the oikonomia term — Paul manages what God has now opened up for public view.
3.10: "The rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms" — the angelic and demonic hierarchies of Second Temple cosmology (Ephesians 1:21; Col 1:16; 1 Enoch 61) are the unwitting audience for the church's existence. Paul's point is that the Jew-Gentile unity of the church is not merely a sociological fact but a cosmic proclamation: the "manifold wisdom" (πολυποίκιλος σοφία) of God — many-colored, variegated — is displayed through the church's unprecedented ethnic reconciliation to the watching cosmic hierarchy.
3.11: "His eternal purpose accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord" — the Greek prothesis tōn aiōnōn (purpose of the ages) uses the same pre-temporal framing as Eph 1:4 and 1:9. God's plan is not improvised in response to human history but executed from a design established before time. This is not fatalism — the plan required historical participants and human response — but it is a claim that the Gentile mission was no accident or afterthought.
3.12: "Boldness and confident access through faith in him" — parrēsia (boldness) was the right of free citizens to speak openly in the assembly — a political right of the politeuma (civic community). It was denied to slaves and foreigners. Prosagōgē (access) was the formal presentation before a monarch or high official — requiring an intermediary patron. Paul presents believers as having both: the free citizen's right of bold speech and the courtier's granted audience before the divine king — through Christ as the patron who effects the introduction.
3.13: "My sufferings for you, which are your glory" — in the Roman patron-client system, a patron's honor normally extended to his clients; equally, a patron's disgrace shamed them. Paul inverts this: his imprisonment (which in Roman eyes dishonors him) actually produces glory for the Gentile communities he serves. This is the logic of the cross applied to apostolic ministry: what the world reads as shame is, within God's economy, the mechanism of honor for others.
3.14: Kneeling was not the standard Jewish posture for prayer. Standing, with hands raised, was the norm (cf. 1 Kgs 8:22; Mark 11:25; Neh 8:6). Kneeling was a posture of deep urgency, penitence, or prostration before a superior (cf. Elijah in 1 Kgs 18:42). Paul's deliberate note that he kneels (γονύ κάμπτω) signals that this is not routine prayer but an act of particular intensity and submission — his own posture enacting the spirit of the petition he is about to offer.
3.15: "From whom every family in heaven and on earth derives its name" — in the ancient world, naming was a covenantal and political act. The one who names exercises authority over what is named (Gen 2:19-20; 17:5). Paul's claim is that all ordered communities — whether human or angelic — derive their relational structure from the divine Father. The Greek patria (family, clan, lineage) shares its root with patēr (Father) — Paul may be exploiting this etymology: every patria is named from the Patēr.
3.16: "Out of his glorious riches" — the repeated "riches" language across Ephesians (1:7, 18; 2:4, 7; 3:8, 16) reflects the imagery of a great patron with vast, inexhaustible benefactions to distribute. In the Greco-Roman patronage system, the patron's generosity was measured by the abundance and continuity of his gifts; those who gave beyond what was expected were celebrated as especially beneficent. Paul applies this framework to God as the ultimate patron whose resources are immeasurable.
3.17: "Rooted and established in love" — Paul deploys two standard ancient metaphors for stability: agricultural (rooted, like a tree whose root system determines its resilience) and architectural (established/founded, like a building on a solid foundation). Both images appear in Hellenistic wisdom literature and Greco-Roman philosophical writing about the virtuous person who cannot be moved by circumstances. Paul's ground for this stability is love — not philosophical training or moral virtue.
3.18: "Wide and long and high and deep" — the four spatial dimensions echo rabbinic and Hellenistic descriptions of the scope of wisdom (Job 11:7-9; Wis 7:24; Sir 24:3-7) and the measurements of the heavenly temple in apocalyptic literature (Ezek 40-48; Rev 21:16). Paul does not explain what the four dimensions measure — the point is vastness beyond measurement. The language deliberately exceeds the categories available to express it.
3.19: "That you may be filled to the measure of all the fullness of God" — the language of divine fullness filling the believer picks up temple imagery: the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34) and the temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11; Ezek 43:5). The eschatological hope was that God's glory would fill all the earth (Hab 2:14; Num 14:21). Paul applies this filling not to a building but to persons — the believer-as-temple theme of Eph 2:22 reaches its climactic expression here.
3.20: The doxology follows a Jewish prayer pattern: acknowledgment of God's incomprehensible greatness, exceeding all human petition or imagination, before the closing ascription of glory. Similar structures appear in Jewish liturgical texts (1QH; Neh 9:5) and throughout the Psalms. "According to his power that is at work within us" — the same dunamis/energeia word pair from 1:19-20, now turned inward: the resurrection power that raised Christ is the same power active inside the praying community.
3.21: "Throughout all generations, for ever and ever" — the Greek phrase εἰς πάσας τὰς γενεὰς τοῦ αἰῶνος τῶν αἰώνων (into all the generations of the age of ages) is an intensified version of the Jewish doxological formula l'olam va'ed (to the age and its age). The multiplication of temporal expressions in the doxology is itself an enactment of the unlimited scope it asserts — human language at its most expansive still falls short of the eternal reality it attempts to describe.

4.1: "A life worthy of the calling" (ἀξίως τῆς κλήσεως) — the concept of living in conformity with one's status was a central concern of Hellenistic moral philosophy. Stoics spoke of living "according to nature" or "according to reason"; Aristotle's ethics centered on virtuous activity consistent with human excellence. Paul takes this form and fills it with a specifically covenantal content: the "calling" is divine election and the vocation it entails, not a philosophical ideal. The ethical imperative (chapters 4-6) rests on the indicative (chapters 1-3) — behavior follows from identity.
4.2: Humility (ταπεινοφροσύνη) was not a virtue in Greco-Roman culture — it was a vice, signifying low social status, servility, and the absence of the proper self-regard that a person of quality should maintain. Cicero, Plutarch, and Aristotle all treat excessive self-deprecation as a defect. Paul's elevation of humility as a virtue to be actively cultivated is one of the most counter-cultural moves in the NT ethical tradition — it derives from Jewish wisdom (Prov 3:34; Isa 57:15) and is embodied in Christ's self-emptying (Phil 2:3-8).
4.3: "The bond of peace" (σύνδεσμος τῆς εἰρήνης) — the syndemos (bond, ligament) appears in Stoic cosmological thought as the rational principle that holds the universe together. Paul takes this philosophical cosmological language and applies it to the church community: peace functions as the structural bond of the new social order. The "unity of the Spirit" is not an achievement to be manufactured but a gift to be guarded — "keep" (τηρεῖν) implies preservation, not creation.
4.4: The seven unities of vv. 4-6 may reflect an early baptismal creed or catechetical formula used in the instruction of converts before baptism. Early Christian baptism was a public confession event at which the candidate declared their faith; the "one Lord, one faith, one baptism" structure mirrors the credal summaries that developed around baptismal practice (cf. 1 Cor 12:13; Gal 3:27-28). "One hope when you were called" — the Greek "one hope of your calling" ties eschatological expectation to the moment of conversion.
4.5: "One Lord, one faith, one baptism" — the terse, staccato rhythm of these three phrases has a creedal quality. "One baptism" distinguished Christian practice from the many ritual washings of the Jewish world (cf. Heb 6:2, "instruction about baptisms" in the plural) and from John's baptism. The singularity of Christian baptism — unrepeatable, marking the irreversible transition of belonging — set it apart. The Didache (an early Christian document, c. AD 80-120) preserves debates about whether immersion or pouring was the proper mode, indicating that this one baptism was taken very seriously as a defining act.
4.6: "One God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all" — the threefold prepositional phrase (hyper, dia, en — over, through, in) echoes Stoic descriptions of the divine Logos permeating all things (cf. Marcus Aurelius; Seneca). Paul takes the Stoic cosmological idiom and applies it to the personal God of Israel. The Jewish confession of the divine unity (Deut 6:4, the Shema: "Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one") underlies the "one God" claim, but now expanded to include the Father's relation to all people in Christ.
4.7: The transition from the seven unities to individual diversity in grace (v. 7) mirrors the Jewish wisdom teaching that God gives different gifts to different persons (Wis 7:7-8:1). Paul's "according to the measure of Christ's gift" presents Christ as the distributing patron who portions out grace-gifts to each member of the community proportionally — not equally, but appropriately.
4.8: The citation of Ps 68:18 introduces a significant tradition-history question. The MT reads "you received gifts from men" (the ascending king receives tribute from the defeated); Paul reads "he gave gifts to men" (the ascending king distributes benefactions). The Syriac Peshitta and Aramaic Targum of Ps 68:18 use a reading closer to Paul's, and the Targum was the standard Aramaic paraphrase used in synagogue worship — a form of the text Paul's Jewish audiences may have known. Whether Paul cites the Targum tradition, interprets the Hebrew with rabbinic flexibility, or follows a pre-existing Christian tradition is debated; what is clear is that the change is deliberate and interpretive.
4.9: The parenthetical explanation of "he ascended" implying a prior descent draws on Jewish cosmological thinking about the relationship between heaven and earth. The debate over what the descent refers to — incarnation (Irenaeus), descent into Hades (some ancient interpreters), or simply descent from heaven to earth at the incarnation — was active in the early church. Jewish apocalyptic literature described prophets ascending to heaven (1 Enoch 14; 2 Enoch 3-7) and returning; Paul's pattern of descent-then-ascent follows this pattern with Christ as the one who moves between realms definitively.
4.10: "In order to fill the whole universe" — tōn ouranōn (the heavens) in the plural echoes Jewish cosmological descriptions of multiple heavens (2 Cor 12:2; 2 Enoch 1-20; T. Levi 2-3), through all of which Christ has ascended to the highest position. The concept of the exalted Lord filling all cosmic space echoes Jewish wisdom traditions where divine Wisdom filled all things (Wis 7:24; Sir 24:3-7); Paul presents Christ as the one who supplants Wisdom as the cosmic ordering principle, now filling all creation from the highest heaven to the lowest earth.
4.11: "Apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers" — the list reflects the early church's functional structure rather than a fixed ecclesiastical hierarchy. Apostles and prophets appear to be itinerant, establishing churches and speaking revelatory words; evangelists similarly mobile; pastors and teachers likely resident in local communities. The single Greek article governing "pastors and teachers" (τοὺς δὲ ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους) has led many commentators to understand these as a single linked role — the "pastor-teacher" — rather than two separate offices.
4.12: "Works of service" (ergon diakoniās) — diakonia (service) carried connotations of servant work in the Greco-Roman social world; it was associated with low status and was not the language of leadership. Paul's elevation of service as the purpose of the gifted leaders — they equip the whole people for diakonia — is counter-cultural. The leaders serve the servants; the goal is the building up of the body, not the display of leadership competence.
4.13: "Mature" (teleios) was a term in Hellenistic philosophy (Stoic, Platonic) for the philosophically perfected person who had achieved the complete development of human reason and virtue — the sage, the Stoic wise man. Paul uses the term for corporate rather than individual maturity: the goal is that "we all" reach teleiotēs together, "in the unity of the faith." The Stoic ideal was the solitary wise man; Paul's goal is a community that together reaches full stature in Christ.
4.14: "Infants, tossed about by the waves" — the image of a ship driven by wind and wave was a standard Hellenistic philosophical metaphor for a person lacking stable wisdom (Plato, Republic 488a-e; Philo, On the Migration of Abraham 148). The technical terms in the next phrase are notable: kubeia (dice-playing) denoted cunning manipulation and sharp practice; methodia tēs planēs (scheming of error/deceit) uses the word for wandering (planē) that gives us "planet" — the wandering stars that were seen as unstable and potentially malevolent. False teaching is described through imagery of cosmic instability.
4.15: "Speaking the truth in love" (ἀληθεύοντες ἐν ἀγάπῃ) — in Hellenistic friendship ethics, frank speech (parrēsia) was the virtue of the true friend who told you what you needed to hear, rather than what you wanted to hear — flattery being the mark of the false friend (Plutarch, How to Tell a Flatterer from a Friend). Paul adopts this framework but fills it with love as the governing motive. The goal of truthful speech is not philosophical correction but the growth of the whole body into Christ.
