4.16: The body metaphor with joints (haphē) and ligaments (syndesmos) engaging each part draws on the ancient medical tradition. Galen and Hippocratic writers described the body's unity through the interdependence of its parts — a wound to one member affects the whole. The Stoics used body-as-cosmos imagery to argue for the interconnectedness of all things. Paul applies this medical and philosophical body-language to the church, but gives it a distinctive head: the body grows only "from him" (ἐξ οὗ — from whom, i.e., from Christ).
4.17: "The futility of their thinking" (ματαιότης τοῦ νοὸς) — the term mataiotēs (futility, vanity) is the word used throughout Ecclesiastes (LXX) for the emptiness of life under the sun. In Jewish polemical tradition, Gentile idolatry was characterized as thinking that leads nowhere — the mind worships what cannot see or speak or act, and is consequently darkened (cf. Wis 13:1-9; Rom 1:21). Paul's "Gentile mind" is a forensic category — what they were before conversion — not a permanent ethnic characteristic.
4.18: "Hardened in heart" (pōrōsis tēs kardias) echoes the OT tradition of divine hardening as judgment on persistent rejection: pharaoh's heart (Exod 9:12); Israel's hardening in Isa 6:10 (cited in all four Gospels and Acts 28). The heart in Hebrew anthropology is the seat of will and moral cognition; a hardened heart is one that has lost the capacity to respond to what it knows. Moral insensitivity and cognitive blindness are two aspects of the same condition — willful rejection leading to incapacity.
4.19: "Having lost all sensitivity" (ἀπηλγηκότες) — the verb means to have become calloused, to have ceased to feel pain. The medical image is precise: a part of the body that has been repeatedly damaged may lose its pain receptors and can no longer register further injury. The Stoics described the morally degraded person similarly — unable to feel the sting of conscience. Having given themselves over to sensuality, these persons have entered a self-reinforcing cycle: insensitivity enables indulgence, which deepens insensitivity.
4.20: "That, however, is not the way of life you learned" — the emphatic "you" (ὑμεῖς δέ) marks the contrast with the Gentile lifestyle just described. The formula "learned Christ" (ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν) is unusual — one learns a teacher's doctrine, not the teacher himself. Paul's phrasing suggests that Christ is not merely the subject of instruction but the content and the teacher simultaneously.
4.21: "Were taught in him in accordance with the truth that is in Jesus" — the vocabulary of learning (μαθεῖν), hearing (ἀκοῦσαι), and being taught (διδαχθῆναι) is the technical language of discipleship and catechesis in the ancient synagogue and early church. The pattern — learn from a teacher, hear the tradition, be formally instructed — was how moral formation worked in both Jewish and Hellenistic education. Paul presents conversion as entry into a comprehensive educational formation, not merely a change of belief.
4.22: "Put off your old self" — the clothing metaphor for moral transformation was common in both Jewish and Hellenistic moral literature. In Jewish tradition, the high priest's garments symbolized moral status (Zech 3:3-5); Isaiah uses clothing for righteousness and salvation (Isa 61:10). In Hellenistic tradition, Stoic writers described the philosopher "putting on" virtue and "taking off" vice. Early Christian baptism was accompanied by an actual change of garment, making the metaphor liturgically enacted as well as conceptually expressed.
4.23: "Be made new in the attitude of your minds" (ἀνανεοῦσθαι τῷ πνεύματι τοῦ νοὸς ὑμῶν) — the renewal of the nous (mind, rational faculty) directly contrasts with the "futility of their thinking" (mataiotēs tou noos) in v. 17. The transformation Paul envisions is cognitive and volitional, not merely behavioral — it is the mental frame that generates the behavior, and it must be continuously renewed (present passive infinitive: ongoing action, not a single event).
4.24: "The new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness" — the "new self" language evokes the creation of humanity in God's image (Gen 1:26-27). The Fall had marred that image; the new creation in Christ restores it. The pair dikaiosunē (righteousness) and hosiotēs (holiness/piety) is the standard Greek ethical pair for justice toward fellow humans and piety toward God — found in Plato, Thucydides, and in Jewish writings (cf. Luke 1:75). Paul's "new self" is not a privatized spiritual state but an embodied social and relational reality.
4.25: "We are all members of one body" — the mele (members) language returns to the body metaphor of vv. 12-16. In Roman legal theory, members of the same corpus (corporation, association) had mutual obligations defined by their shared membership. Paul grounds the obligation to truthful speech not in general ethics but in ecclesial identity: lying to fellow church members is like a body part deceiving itself.
4.26: "In your anger do not sin" is a citation of Ps 4:4 (LXX). The Psalm was an evening prayer (its heading notes it was "for the director of music"), and the following verse about "while you are on your beds" supports reading this as advice not to let anger carry over through the night. The principle "do not let the sun go down on your anger" may echo Deut 24:15, which forbids withholding a laborer's wages overnight — a similar principle of not allowing unfulfilled obligations to accumulate past the day's end.
4.27: "Do not give the devil a foothold" — topos (place, opportunity) is both a military and a legal term: a topos is a strategic position in battle, or a ground for legal action. The image is tactical: allowing anger to persist overnight gives the adversary an established position from which to launch further operations. The identification of the adversary as "the devil" (ὁ διάβολος) uses the Greek term for the accuser or slanderer — the one who operates through accusation and deception.
4.28: Manual labor was despised by the social elite in Greco-Roman society as incompatible with the dignity of a gentleman. Cicero (De Officiis I.150-151) lists tradesmen and manual workers among those whose occupation "cannot be gentlemanly" because it requires selling labor. Jewish tradition had a different evaluation — the rabbis worked trades alongside teaching (Paul himself was a tentmaker). Paul's elevation of hand-work as the means of earning and of giving to those in need is counter-cultural relative to Greco-Roman social values.
4.29: "Unwholesome talk" — sapros (rotten, decayed) was used for food that had spoiled and for wood that had rotted. Applied to speech, it signals talk that corrupts rather than nourishes. The positive alternative — speech that "ministers grace" to hearers — uses the charis vocabulary that runs through Ephesians: even conversation participates in the economy of grace or its opposite. The "need" of the hearer, not the preference of the speaker, determines what is appropriate.
4.30: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit" — the background is Isa 63:10: "they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit." Isaiah's account of Israel's rebellion in the wilderness becomes Paul's warning to the Ephesian community. The verb lypeite (grieve) implies that the Spirit is a personal agent who can be hurt by behavior — one grieves a person, not a force or an influence. "Sealed for the day of redemption" returns to the seal language of 1:13-14: the Spirit's presence is the mark of divine ownership, guaranteed until the final redemption.
4.31: "Bitterness, rage, anger, brawling, slander, along with every form of malice" — this vice list (pikria, thymos, orgē, kraugē, blasphēmia, kakia) follows a conventional Hellenistic and Jewish pattern of moral instruction. Vice and virtue lists were standard teaching forms in Stoic diatribe, Jewish wisdom literature (Wis 14:25-26; T. Gad 5:1), and early Christian catechesis (Col 3:8; Gal 5:19-21). The listing itself was a mnemonic form, intended to be memorized and reviewed in moral self-examination.
4.32: "Forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you" — Hellenistic moral philosophy encouraged forgiveness as a virtue of the magnanimous person: the truly great-souled (μεγαλόψυχος) person does not hold grudges because they consider offenses beneath their dignity. Paul's ground is entirely different: forgiveness is grounded in the prior forgiveness received from God, not in personal nobility or the insignificance of the offense. The "just as" (καθώς) is normative and specific — the scope and character of divine forgiveness in Christ sets the pattern for human forgiveness.
